summary: The Docker-based reproduction clones the vulnerable upstream release, sets a dearmor size limit of 1 byte, and still successfully dearmors an 11-byte armored body, proving that armored body bytes bypass `DearmorOptions::limit`.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should ignore a 1-byte dearmor limit and still print `DEARMOR_LIMIT_BYPASS_OK:hello world`, demonstrating that the advertised dearmor body-size guard is not enforced.
actual_observed_result: Running `./reproduction/run.sh` on 2026-06-03 cloned commit `24b8cc906d8358a5591510655e1081e2b034d5c6`, built the helper container, and printed `DEARMOR_LIMIT_BYPASS_OK:hello world`. The wrapper script verified that signal and emitted `DEARMOR_LIMIT_BYPASS_CONFIRMED`.
verification_method: The helper crate invokes `Dearmor::with_options(..., DearmorOptions::default().set_limit(1))` on a small armored message and prints a fixed success marker only if dearmoring still succeeds. The host run script greps for that marker to distinguish a real limit bypass from build or runtime failures.
evidence_produced_by_run: The host wrapper emitted `DEARMOR_LIMIT_BYPASS_CONFIRMED` after matching the bypass marker.
limitations_or_blockers: The PoC uses a 1-byte configured limit to keep the demonstration small and fast; it exercises the same unchecked body-read path that also defeats the default 1 GiB limit.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container.
